raw_response:
# SOC 2 Readiness Assessment — Privacy Notice Gap Analysis
## Control: P1.1 — Privacy Notice Accuracy and Completeness
**Entity:** Helix Software GmbH | **Assessor Review Date:** 2025 | **Policy Version:** 3.2

---

## Executive Summary

Cross-referencing the external Privacy Policy (v3.2) against the ROPA reveals **material contradictions and omissions** that undermine the accuracy of disclosures made to data subjects. The most serious finding is a direct factual contradiction regarding third-party data sharing. Several undisclosed processors, an international data transfer, and a missing retention policy compound the risk. In aggregate, these gaps represent a meaningful failure of transparency obligations under GDPR Articles 13/14, and directly threaten SOC 2 Privacy Criteria compliance under P1.1.

**Total Findings: 8** | Critical: 2 | High: 3 | Medium: 2 | Low: 1

---

## Findings

---

### Finding 1: Privacy Policy Directly Contradicts Actual Third-Party Data Sharing Practices

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Finding ID** | GAP-001 |
| **Severity** | 🔴 Critical |
| **Policy Section** | Section 4 — Data Sharing |
| **ROPA Reference** | PROC-008 (Amplitude), PROC-009 (SendGrid), PROC-010 (Salesforce), PROC-007 (OneTrust) |

**Gap Description:**

Section 4 of the Privacy Policy contains the following unambiguous statement:

> *"Your data remains exclusively within the Helix Software environment."*

This is factually false. The ROPA identifies **four active third-party processors** who receive personal data from Helix Software:

- **Amplitude Inc.** (PROC-008) — receives User ID, session ID, IP address, device fingerprint, feature usage events, click paths, and more
- **SendGrid / Twilio** (PROC-009) — receives email addresses, names, email content, and delivery metadata
- **Salesforce Inc.** (PROC-010) — receives customer names, emails, usage metrics, support history, and NPS scores
- **OneTrust LLC** (PROC-007) — receives consent preferences, truncated IP addresses, and timestamps

The policy does acknowledge Stripe (PROC-002) for payment processing, which is the **sole exception** where third-party processing is disclosed. All other processors are entirely absent from the policy's data sharing section.

**Why This Matters:**

This is not an omission — it is an **affirmative misrepresentation** to data subjects. Individuals reading the privacy policy are told their data stays exclusively within Helix's environment, while in practice it is being transmitted to at least four external organizations. This directly violates GDPR Article 13(1)(e), which requires disclosure of recipients or categories of recipients of personal data. It also creates significant exposure under SOC 2 P1.1, which requires that notice accurately reflect actual practices.

---

### Finding 2: Undisclosed International Data Transfer to the United States

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Finding ID** | GAP-002 |
| **Severity** | 🔴 Critical |
| **Policy Section** | Section 6 — Data Location |
| **ROPA Reference** | PROC-008 (Amplitude Inc.) |

**Gap Description:**

Section 6 of the Privacy Policy states:

> *"All personal data is processed and stored within the European Economic Area."*
> *"We do not transfer personal data outside the EEA."*

The ROPA directly contradicts this. PROC-008 records that Amplitude Inc. processes data in the **United States (us-east-1)**, with the transfer safeguard listed as **Standard Contractual Clauses (2021)**. Personal data categories transferred include User ID, session ID, IP address, device fingerprint, feature usage events, page views, and click paths — a substantial and sensitive dataset by volume and behavioral richness.

**Why This Matters:**

This is a second affirmative misrepresentation. Data subjects are told no transfers outside the EEA occur. In reality, detailed behavioral and technical data is being exported to the United States. Under GDPR Article 13(1)(f), the existence of international transfers and the safeguards relied upon must be disclosed. While SCCs appear to be in place (providing a partial compliance mitigant), the **complete absence of disclosure** to data subjects is a separate and standalone violation. The combination of this finding with GAP-001 suggests a systemic gap in keeping the privacy policy synchronized with operational reality.

---

### Finding 3: No Retention Policy Defined for Product Analytics Data — Policy States Finite Retention for All Data

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Finding ID** | GAP-003 |
| **Severity** | 🔴 High |
| **Policy Section** | Section 5 — Data Retention |
| **ROPA Reference** | PROC-008 (Amplitude Inc.) |

**Gap Description:**

Section 5 of the Privacy Policy states that personal data is retained for the duration of account activity plus 24 months, after which it is *"permanently deleted using industry-standard secure deletion methods."* This implies a universal and finite retention schedule covering all personal data Helix processes.

However, PROC-008 in the ROPA explicitly records the retention period for Amplitude analytics data as:

> *"Indefinite (no deletion policy configured)"*

This means behavioral and technical data — including IP addresses, device fingerprints, and detailed user activity — is being retained with **no defined end date** in a third-party system, directly contradicting the finite retention schedule communicated to data subjects. Additionally, this raises GDPR Article 5(1)(e) storage limitation concerns independently of the notice gap.

**Why This Matters:**

Data subjects have a reasonable expectation, based on the published policy, that their data will be deleted within a bounded timeframe. That expectation is incorrect for a material processing activity. The "no deletion policy configured" note in the ROPA also indicates this is an operational gap, not merely a documentation issue — meaning data may be accumulating in Amplitude without any lifecycle controls.

---

### Finding 4: Behavioral Tracking and Analytics Processing Not Disclosed in Privacy Policy

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Finding ID** | GAP-004 |
| **Severity** | 🔴 High |
| **Policy Section** | Section 2 — Information We Collect; Section 3 — How We Use Your Information |
| **ROPA Reference** | PROC-008 (Amplitude Inc.) |

**Gap Description:**

PROC-008 documents collection of a rich set of behavioral data including: device fingerprints, click paths, time-on-page, page views, session IDs, and feature usage events — processed by Amplitude for "product improvement and feature adoption analysis."

Reviewing the Privacy Policy:

- **Section 2** references "Platform interaction data: features accessed, project activity logs, notification preferences" under automatically collected data — but omits **device fingerprints**, **click paths**, **time-on-page**, and **page views**, which are meaningfully more invasive data points.
- **Section 3** references "Analyzing aggregated usage patterns" for product improvement — but the ROPA indicates this is not purely aggregated; it involves **individual-level behavioral tracking** tied to User ID and session ID.
- There is **no disclosure** that this processing is conducted via a third-party analytics platform.

**Why This Matters:**

The privacy policy materially understates both the granularity of data collected and the nature of how it is processed. Describing individual-level behavioral tracking as "aggregated usage patterns" is misleading. Data subjects cannot exercise meaningful rights (e.g., objection under Art. 21, or erasure under Art. 17) over processing they have not been accurately informed about.

---

### Finding 5: CRM Processing of Customer Data Not Disclosed to Data Subjects

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Finding ID** | GAP-005 |
| **Severity** | 🔴 High |
| **Policy Section** | Section 3 — How We Use Your Information; Section 4 — Data Sharing |
| **ROPA Reference** | PROC-010 (Salesforce Inc.) |

**Gap Description:**

PROC-010 documents that customer personal data — including name, email, company, job title, subscription tier, usage metrics, support history, renewal dates, and NPS scores — is transferred to **Salesforce** and used for "sales pipeline management and customer success tracking."

Neither the purpose (sales pipeline management) nor the processor (Salesforce) appears anywhere in the Privacy Policy. Section 3 lists approved purposes for processing but does not include sales operations or CRM activities. Section 4 does not list Salesforce as a recipient.

**Why This Matters:**

Sales pipeline management and customer success tracking constitute distinct processing purposes that go beyond service delivery, support, and billing — the purposes disclosed in the policy. Data subjects are not informed that their behavioral data, support history, and renewal information are being used to inform commercial activities. Under GDPR Article 13(1)(c) and (e), both the purpose and the recipient must be disclosed.

---

### Finding 6: Application Logging Processing Activity Absent from Privacy Policy

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Finding ID** | GAP-006 |
| **Severity** | 🟡 Medium |
| **Policy Section** | Section 2 — Information We Collect |
| **ROPA Reference** | PROC-005 (Application Logging) |

**Gap Description:**

PROC-005 documents the collection of API request metadata, error traces, user IDs, and IP addresses for system reliability and debugging purposes, with a 12-month retention period.

The Privacy Policy does not explicitly disclose application-level logging as a processing activity. Section 2's "Platform interaction data" and Section 4's security language do not clearly capture API request metadata and error traces. The retention period of 12 months for this data is also not reconcilable with the policy's general retention statement of "active account + 24 months," meaning the policy is inconsistent with the actual shorter retention applied here (though in this case the discrepancy is favorable to the data subject).

**Why This Matters:**

While application logging is a standard practice, data subjects are entitled to know that API-level interaction data and error traces containing user identifiers are captured and retained. Omitting this processing activity from the notice, even if the data is internally held, is an incomplete disclosure under GDPR Article 13.

---

### Finding 7: Cookie Consent Management Processor (OneTrust) Not Referenced in Privacy Policy

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Finding ID** | GAP-007 |
| **Severity** | 🟡 Medium |
| **Policy Section** | Section 4 — Data Sharing |
| **ROPA Reference** | PROC-007 (OneTrust LLC) |

**Gap Description:**

PROC-007 documents that OneTrust LLC processes consent preferences, truncated IP addresses, and consent timestamps for website visitors. OneTrust is hosted within the EEA (Ireland), and a DPA is in place.

The Privacy Policy makes no mention of OneTrust or of consent management as a processing activity. There is no reference to the collection of consent preference data or its transmission to a third-party consent management platform.

**Why This Matters:**

While the risk level is lower given EEA hosting and a DPA in place, data subjects visiting the website are not informed that their consent choices are recorded and managed via a third-party platform. This is a minor but addressable transparency gap. A cookie policy or banner supplementing the privacy policy would be the typical remediation path, but the gap should be documented.

---

### Finding 8: ROPA Last Review Date for Amplitude Lags Other Records by Nine Months

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Finding ID** | GAP-008 |
| **Severity** | 🟢 Low |
| **Policy Section** | N/A (Governance / ROPA Maintenance) |
| **ROPA Reference** | PROC-008 (Amplitude Inc.) |

**Gap Description:**

All other active ROPA entries carry a `last_reviewed` date of **2025-01-15**. PROC-008 (Amplitude) shows a `last_reviewed` date of **2024-03-15** — the same date the DPA was signed — suggesting it has **not been reviewed since onboarding**. This is the same record that contains the indefinite retention problem and the undisclosed US transfer, raising concern that these issues have not been subject to periodic oversight.

**Why This Matters:**

While this is a governance observation rather than a direct P1.1 notice gap, the stale review date for the highest-risk processing record indicates that ROPA maintenance controls are not being applied consistently. Under GDPR Article 30 and SOC 2 P6/P8 criteria, periodic review of processing records is expected. The absence of review for this specific record correlates directly with the most serious findings in this assessment.

---

## Summary Table

| ID | Title | Severity | Policy Section | ROPA Ref |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GAP-001 | Privacy policy directly contradicts actual third-party data sharing | 🔴 Critical | §4 Data Sharing | PROC-007, 008, 009, 010 |
| GAP-002 | Undisclosed international data transfer to the United States | 🔴 Critical | §6 Data Location | PROC-008 |
| GAP-003 | No retention policy for analytics data; policy implies universal finite retention | 🔴 High | §5 Data Retention | PROC-008 |
| GAP-004 | Behavioral tracking scope and nature materially understated | 🔴 High | §2, §3 | PROC-008 |
| GAP-005 | CRM processing and Salesforce not disclosed to data subjects | 🔴 High | §3, §4 | PROC-010 |
| GAP-006 | Application logging not disclosed in privacy notice | 🟡 Medium | §2 | PROC-005 |
| GAP-007 | OneTrust consent processor not referenced in privacy policy | 🟡 Medium | §4 | PROC-007 |
| GAP-008 | ROPA review date for Amplitude stale; not reviewed since onboarding | 🟢 Low | N/A | PROC-008 |

---

## Recommended Remediation Priorities

1. **Immediate (before SOC 2 audit window):** Revise Section 4 and Section 6 of the Privacy Policy to remove the false "exclusively within Helix" and "no transfers outside EEA" statements and replace with accurate disclosures of all active processors and the US transfer via SCCs.

2. **Short-term (within 30 days):** Define and implement a retention schedule within Amplitude. Update Section 5 of the privacy policy to accurately reflect per-activity retention periods or provide a retention schedule appendix.

3. **Short-term:** Expand Section 2 and Section 3 to accurately describe individual-level behavioral tracking, and explicitly name or categorize third-party analytics, email delivery, and CRM as processing activities and recipient categories.

4. **Medium-term:** Conduct a full ROPA-to-policy reconciliation review on a defined periodic schedule (recommended: semi-annually) to prevent future drift between operational practices and published notices.
